Home / Financial / Funding a Custody Case
Financial

Affording the fight for your kids.

Custody cases are expensive. But there's a full range of options between 'full-price attorney' and 'going it alone' — and most dads don't know half of them.

The cost of a custody case can range from a few hundred dollars (if both parties agree and you handle the paperwork yourselves) to tens of thousands (contested cases with hearings, evaluations, and motions). Most cases fall somewhere in between. Here's how to navigate that range without going broke.

The single biggest cost driver is conflict. Every motion, every hearing, every exchange of letters between attorneys costs money. An agreement — even a partial one — dramatically reduces costs. That doesn't mean giving in; it means being strategic about what you fight over.

Free: legal aid

If your income is below roughly 125–200% of the federal poverty level, you may qualify for free legal representation through a legal aid organization. This is real representation — attorneys, not just advice.

  • Quality varies by organization and by how busy they are.
  • Not all legal aid offices handle family law — call and ask specifically about custody cases.
  • Find your local legal aid office through LawHelp.org or the LSC legal aid finder.

Low-cost: law school family law clinics

Many accredited law schools run clinics where supervised law students handle real cases at no cost or significantly reduced rates. The supervision is real — a licensed attorney oversees every step.

  • Search "[your city] law school family law clinic" to find one near you.
  • These clinics often have capacity limits and waitlists, so apply early.
  • Cases handled by clinics can take longer, but the representation is often very good.

Low-cost: limited scope representation

Also called "unbundled" legal services — you hire an attorney for specific tasks rather than full representation. For example:

  • A one-hour consultation to understand your situation and options ($100–300)
  • Attorney drafts your parenting plan; you file it yourself
  • Attorney coaches you before a hearing but doesn't appear with you
  • Attorney reviews a proposed agreement before you sign it

This can get you professional guidance on the most important decisions without paying for full representation throughout. Not all attorneys offer this — ask directly when you call.

Going it alone: pro se (self-representation)

In uncontested cases where both parents agree on the basics, self-representation is genuinely viable. Courts see it regularly and most have resources specifically for self-represented litigants.

  • Self-help centers: most family courts have a self-help center or facilitator's office with staff who can answer procedural questions and help with forms (they can't give legal advice, but they can help you file correctly).
  • State court websites: most states publish the official forms and instructions for custody and parenting plan filings.
  • Online tools: LawHelp.org has state-specific guides for self-represented parents in family court.

Self-representation is harder in contested cases — if the other parent has an attorney, you are at a significant disadvantage. Get at least a consultation before deciding to go fully alone.

Paying for a full attorney: how to make it work

If you need full representation and don't qualify for legal aid, there are still ways to manage the cost:

  • Payment plans: many family-law attorneys offer payment plans. Ask directly — it's not an unusual request. Expect a retainer (upfront deposit) regardless.
  • Fee waivers for court costs: filing fees, service fees, and other court costs can be waived if you meet income requirements. Ask the court clerk for a fee waiver application. This doesn't cover attorney fees, but it removes one cost.
  • Shop around: consultation fees vary widely, and so do hourly rates. A less experienced attorney at a lower rate who handles family law regularly can outperform a more expensive generalist.
  • Narrow the scope: the more you and the other parent can agree on outside of court, the less you spend on attorney time. A mediator (typically $100–300/hour, split between both parties) can often facilitate agreement on the sticking points cheaper than litigation.

What you're actually paying for

Understanding what drives cost helps you control it:

  • Filing fees: $100–400 depending on your state, waivable with low income.
  • Service of process: formally serving the other parent. $50–150.
  • Attorney time: the big variable. Every phone call, email, motion, and hearing is billed at your attorney's hourly rate ($150–400+ depending on location and experience).
  • Guardian ad litem: in some contested cases, the court appoints a GAL to represent the children's interests. This cost is often split between the parents. Can run $1,000–5,000+.
  • Custody evaluation: a formal evaluation by a psychologist or social worker ordered in high-conflict cases. Can run $3,000–10,000. Avoid if at all possible — try to reach agreement first.

Document everything, from the start. Regardless of your legal representation level, keeping a simple log of your time with your kids, communications with the other parent, and any relevant incidents costs you nothing and can be invaluable. Courts and attorneys work with documentation.

For a broader view of legal resources available to you as a single dad, see the Finding Legal Help guide in the Legal section.

This page is general information, not legal advice. Costs and procedures vary significantly by state and by the complexity of your case. Consult a licensed family-law attorney about your specific situation.
